items.front: When may the word takeoff be used in ATC phraseology?
items.back: Only to actually clear an aircraft for takeoff or to cancel a takeoff clearance.

items.front: What words should replace takeoff in other departure instructions?
items.back: Words such as depart, departure, or fly.

items.front: Is takeoff appropriate in a heading instruction such as runway heading?
items.back: No.

items.front: May takeoff be used when canceling a takeoff clearance?
items.back: Yes.

items.front: What simple substitute fits a route or heading instruction instead of takeoff?
items.back: Fly.

items.front: What must always be included when a departure clearance is relayed through an FSS or dispatcher?
items.back: The airport of departure.

items.front: When the clearance limit is an airport, what must follow the airport name?
items.back: The word airport.

items.front: When the clearance limit is a NAVAID and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of NAVAID.

items.front: When the clearance limit is an intersection or waypoint and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of intersection or waypoint.

items.front: How is the destination handled for Air Force One operations?
items.back: Do not specify the destination airport; use destination as filed.

items.front: What happens if an aircraft is vectored off a published SID or ODP?
items.back: The DP is canceled and ATC becomes responsible for separation from terrain and obstructions.

items.front: When a location without ATC service is within a Class E surface area and a turn or heading is needed after departure, what must be obtained first?
items.back: The pilot's concurrence.

items.front: At all other airports, what should not be specified after takeoff?
items.back: Direction of takeoff or turn after takeoff.

items.front: What phrase is used when a SID contains published crossing restrictions and an interim altitude must be issued?
items.back: Climb via SID except maintain (altitude).

items.front: What route condition must be met before an abbreviated departure clearance can be used?
items.back: The filed route must not have been changed before departure.

items.front: What must all ATC facilities concerned have for an abbreviated departure clearance to work?
items.back: Sufficient route-of-flight information to exercise their control responsibilities.

items.front: Before an IFR flight departs under abbreviated procedures, what information must be relayed between the facilities concerned?
items.back: Destination airport information.

items.front: What altitude item must still be stated in an abbreviated departure clearance?
items.back: The assigned altitude.

items.front: What does FRC mean?
items.back: Full Route Clearance Necessary.

items.front: If FRC followed by a fix appears, how far must the full route clearance be issued?
items.back: To the specified fix.

items.front: What is the core no-change abbreviated phrase with a SID?
items.back: Cleared to (destination) airport, (SID) departure, then as filed.

items.front: When should abbreviated departure clearance procedures not be used?
items.back: When the pilot requests a detailed clearance or the operation requires special military handling such as ALTRV.

items.front: What release control must an untowered IFR departure receive?
items.back: A departure release, a hold for release, or a release time.

items.front: What does hold for release mean?
items.back: The departure clearance is not valid until additional instructions are received.

items.front: What does a release time specify?
items.back: The earliest time an aircraft may depart.

items.front: When issuing a release time using a specified clock time, what else must be given?
items.back: A time check.

items.front: Does a release time using a specified number of minutes require a time check?
items.back: No.

items.front: When CFR is in effect, what is the normal airborne window around the assigned time?
items.back: From 2 minutes before to 1 minute after the assigned time, unless otherwise coordinated.

items.front: What extra instruction must accompany a clearance void time at an airport without an operating control tower?
items.back: Require the pilot to advise ATC of intentions no later than 30 minutes after the void time if not airborne.

items.front: What does an expired clearance void time do?
items.back: It withdraws authority to depart IFR until a new release is issued and acknowledged; it does not cancel the departure clearance or IFR flight plan.

items.front: What approval is required before releasing an aircraft affected by a ground stop?
items.back: Approval from the originator of the ground stop.

items.front: May the aircraft be released just because the receiving facility can accept it?
items.back: No.

items.front: Who controls any exception to the ground stop for that aircraft?
items.back: The originator of the ground stop.

items.front: What is the default action when the ground stop remains active and no exception has been approved?
items.back: Keep the aircraft on the ground.
